metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a draft of the none/low/medium/high level definitions for the new binary to the plan body so the executor has a canonical reference and AC1 is verifiable against specific prose.
findings.finding: AC1 requires 'clear none/low/medium/high definitions' for the new binary in the verify prompt, but the plan provides no draft of these definitions. An executor must invent the definitions from scratch, creating a real design decision that is deferred under an apparently concrete AC.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the What section: 'none = the changed path has comprehensive automated test coverage; low = partial coverage (happy path only); medium = coverage exists but is import-skipped in a standard CI env; high = the changed path has no automated test coverage in any CI configuration.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `tests/unit/test_impact_model_versioning.py` to the edit-target list and update the `== "code-v2"` assertion on line 34 to `== "code-v3"` (or make it read the constant dynamically) when bumping `IMPACT_MODEL_VERSION`.
findings.finding: The plan does not enumerate `tests/unit/test_impact_model_versioning.py` as a file requiring update, yet line 34 contains a hardcoded assertion `== "code-v2"` that will fail the moment `IMPACT_MODEL_VERSION` is bumped to `code-v3` in `sidecar.py`.
findings.location: AC: 'make test passes on the change' / tests/unit/test_impact_model_versioning.py:34
findings.suggested_fix: Change `assert payload["impact_model_version"] == sidecar.IMPACT_MODEL_VERSION == "code-v2"` to `assert payload["impact_model_version"] == sidecar.IMPACT_MODEL_VERSION == "code-v3"`, or better: `assert payload["impact_model_version"] == sidecar.IMPACT_MODEL_VERSION` (removing the string literal so future bumps don't re-break it).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `tests/unit/fixtures/code_review_impact_labels.jsonl` to the edit-target list and include at least one labeled row with `reachable_path_without_automated_coverage: true` (label `HIGH`) to exercise the new binary through the existing A/B gate.
findings.finding: The plan's AC3 requires replaying a #529-shaped finding and recording its new impact in a test fixture, but the fixture file `tests/unit/fixtures/code_review_impact_labels.jsonl` — which both `test_impact_code.py` and `ab_impact_model.py` consume — is not named as an edit target. A new row with the `reachable_path_without_automated_coverage` binary set is needed for the replay assertion to work, and omitting it means the A/B gate (`test_ab_absolute_gate_passes_for_current_model`) may silently pass without ever exercising the new binary.
findings.location: AC: 'A unit test replays a #529-shaped finding and asserts impact > 0 under code-v3'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify in the AC and verify prompt that the new field is a `bool` (True/False), consistent with all other consequence binaries in `CodeSeverityAttrs`; remove the 'none/low/medium/high' vocabulary from the AC and the prompt definition for this field.
findings.finding: AC line 1 specifies 'clear none/low/medium/high definitions' for the new binary, but consequence binaries in the existing CodeSeverityAttrs (verify.py:325–365) and _CODE_MAINT_BINARIES (decide.py:198–205) are bool fields, not ordinal enums. 'none/low/medium/high' is the ordinal vocabulary for severity attributes (prod_impact, debt_impact, etc.). An implementer following the AC as written could implement the new field as an ordinal string, which would cause _code_truthy() to silently return False for every non-empty string value, contributing 0 to the lane score regardless of the LLM's output.
findings.suggested_fix: Change AC line 1 to: 'clear true/false semantics (TRUE = a reachable code path introduced or unmasked by the diff has no automated coverage)'. Mirror the existing bool-field pattern in both verify.py and code-review-verify.md.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `_CODE_MAINT_BINARIES` in `decide.py` (with the new binary name and its tier value) to the plan's explicit list of required edit sites.
findings.finding: The plan's 'What' section and all AC lines name 'the code-review verification model + verify prompt' as the edit sites for the new binary, but never name `_CODE_MAINT_BINARIES` in `decide.py:198-205` — the dict that assigns a tier value and makes a binary participate in `_code_lane_severity()`. Adding the field only to `verify.py` and `code-review-verify.md` leaves the binary in the schema but silently scored 0 forever, because `_code_lane_severity` iterates exclusively over `_CODE_MAINT_BINARIES.items()`.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a bullet to the What section: 'Add `"reachable_path_without_automated_coverage": _CODE_TIER_MODERATE` to `_CODE_MAINT_BINARIES` in `src/rebar/llm/review_kernel/decide.py`.' The AC replay test (AC line 3) will catch this if the fixture asserts impact > 0.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace 'e.g. reachable_path_without_automated_coverage' with the decided field name so the schema field, verify-prompt variable, and test fixture all have a single canonical anchor.
findings.finding: The binary field name is given as 'e.g. reachable_path_without_automated_coverage' — 'e.g.' signals the name is not decided, leaving the executor to choose an identifier that flows into the model schema, the verify prompt, and the unit-test fixture with no canonical anchor.
findings.suggested_fix: State the decided name directly, e.g.: 'Add a boolean field `untested_reachable_path` (tier moderate) to the code-review verification model and the verify prompt.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a concrete proving mechanism for the 'byte-unchanged' sub-claim, e.g. a snapshot test or an assertion that `compute_impact(v2_sidecar_fixture)` equals a pinned expected value, and reference it in AC3.
findings.finding: AC3 requires that the 'code-v2 path is byte-unchanged for old sidecars', but no proving command or test assertion mechanism is specified for the byte-unchanged sub-claim; the described unit test only asserts 'impact > 0' under code-v3.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria, AC3: '...while the code-v2 path is byte-unchanged for old sidecars'
findings.suggested_fix: Extend AC3: '...a second assertion feeds a v2-tagged sidecar fixture through the unchanged compute_impact code path and asserts the output equals a pinned snapshot (e.g. `assert result == PINNED_V2_IMPACT`).  The snapshot is committed alongside the test.'

findings.finding: No file_impact declared on a leaf work ticket.
findings.suggested_fix: Record the {path, reason} files this work will touch (e.g. via set_file_impact) so the attestation is scoped to them.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC requiring a unit test that asserts a #529-shaped finding with the new binary absent/abstained scores identically (or no higher) to the code-v2 baseline, verifying the abstain-safe default does not inflate.
findings.finding: The plan mandates the new binary has an abstain-safe default ('missing -> 0, never inflates') as a core safety invariant, but no AC requires a test verifying that when the field is absent or abstained the score does NOT inflate — only the positive path (impact > 0 when field is set) has a required test.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — third bullet ('A unit test replays a #529-shaped finding...')
findings.suggested_fix: Add a second assertion in the unit test (or a separate test case): construct the same #529 finding but omit the new `reachable_path_without_automated_coverage` field, run it through the code-v3 model, and assert the resulting impact equals the code-v2 score (i.e., still 0.0 or at most the pre-existing baseline).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify in AC3 which attribute combination the #529-shaped fixture row must include (e.g., whether escapes_automation=true is required) and confirm the existing test_impact_code_separates_high_from_nit separation invariant still holds with the new row added.
findings.finding: The plan says 'replay #529's finding through the extended model and record its new impact in a test fixture', but at moderate tier + cold churn (churn90=0) + without escapes_automation, the new binary yields impact=0.24 — below the existing NIT ceiling of 0.30 enforced by test_impact_code_separates_high_from_nit. If the implementer adds the #529-shaped row to code_review_impact_labels.jsonl with cold-churn attributes (the scenario that actually fired), the fixture HIGH-class median may not clear the >0.30 NIT ceiling or the >0.30 separation gap, breaking the existing regression test. The plan does not specify which attribute combination the fixture row will use, leaving the AC3 test and AC6 (make test passes) in an unresolved tension.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC3: 'A unit test replays a #529-shaped finding and asserts impact > 0 under code-v3'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC that verifies the version-segmented cohort exists (or is scheduled) before code-v3 impact scores are consumed by any threshold, per the ADR 0036 requirement stated in the What section.
findings.finding: The 'What' section declares a hard REQUIREMENT per ADR 0036: 'a fresh version-segmented cohort before any threshold consumes it', but no Acceptance Criterion covers or verifies this requirement. It is described work with no covering criterion.
findings.location: What section (ADR 0036 sentence) vs Acceptance Criteria (AC1–AC6)
findings.suggested_fix: Add: '- [ ] A version-segmented cohort for code-v3 is established (or gated) so that code-v3 impact scores are not consumed by thresholds calibrated on code-v2 data, per ADR 0036.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit rollback/back-out plan (e.g., revert sidecar version field to code-v2, feature-flag the new binary, or a documented step to re-pin the old model) so the schema change is a reversible, justified choice.
findings.finding: The plan changes the impact model schema (new binary field) and bumps IMPACT_MODEL_VERSION but provides no explicit rollback/back-out plan if code-v3 causes unexpected scoring regressions. The plan acknowledges forward compat (abstain-safe default, code-v2 byte-unchanged) but T4 requires an explicit reversibility mechanism, not just acknowledgement.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add or update an ADR (or CLAUDE.md design-doc section) recording the decision to introduce `reachable_path_without_automated_coverage`, the rationale for tier=moderate, and the none/low/medium/high boundary semantics.
findings.finding: The plan introduces a novel architectural/vocabulary decision — a new maint-lane binary (`reachable_path_without_automated_coverage`, tier moderate) added to the impact model — but does not create or update an ADR to record WHY this dimension was chosen, what tier was selected and why, and what the none/low/medium/high boundary semantics mean. ADR 0036 is cited only for the version-bump obligation, not as the record of this new design decision.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a bullet under '## What': 'Update or append to ADR 0036 (or create ADR 00XX) documenting the new binary: motivation (the #529 incident), chosen tier and rationale, abstain-safe default justification, and semantic boundary definitions.' This satisfies T5e(c) and T7(a).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm (in the plan or AC) that `tier moderate` is wired through the existing named-tier system / constant rather than a hardcoded literal, or note it as a configurable parameter.
findings.finding: The plan asserts 'tier moderate' for the new binary as a fixed value without indicating whether this weight follows a configurable tier registry or is a hardcoded literal in decide.py. Since tier weights are impact-model thresholds expected to evolve as the scoring system is calibrated, the plan should explicitly state whether this value is configurable (via the existing tier system) or will require a code change to tune.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a RETIRE clause for code-v2 sidecars: specify when they age out of cohort queries (e.g., after the code-v3 cohort reaches N samples), who owns the cleanup/migration, and how the version-segmentation filter is enforced at read time.
findings.finding: The plan bumps IMPACT_MODEL_VERSION (code-v2 → code-v3) and states old sidecars remain byte-unchanged, but does not define a RETIRE path for accumulated code-v2 sidecar records: who owns the migration, when (if ever) old-version sidecars age out of the cohort, and whether they accumulate indefinitely in storage.
findings.location: ## What — 'Per ADR 0036 a formula/vocabulary change REQUIRES an IMPACT_MODEL_VERSION bump … version-segmented cohort'; AC line 'IMPACT_MODEL_VERSION is bumped to code-v3 and sidecars carry it.'
findings.suggested_fix: In the plan's 'What' section, add: 'code-v2 sidecars are excluded from threshold-tuning queries via a WHERE version = 'code-v3' filter (already implied by version-segmented cohort). After the code-v3 cohort reaches [N] samples, a cleanup job (or TTL policy) archives/deletes code-v2 records. Ownership: same sidecar maint lane.' Reference this in the AC as a new bullet.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a one-sentence positive rationale for placing the new binary in the maint lane at moderate tier (vs serious, given safety_net_removal_without_replacement precedent and the red-main severity of the #529 incident), so the tier choice is reviewable and the implementer understands the design intent.
findings.finding: The plan asserts 'tier moderate' and maint-lane placement for the new binary but gives no positive rationale for either choice. safety_net_removal_without_replacement (the closest analogue, covering a related safety-net gap) is serious-tier (0.9), and the #529 incident produced a red-main incident 57 minutes after merge — an outcome that could justify serious-tier. Without a stated rationale the implementer has no basis to resist pressure to adjust the tier or lane, and reviewers cannot evaluate whether moderate vs serious is correct.
findings.suggested_fix: After naming the binary, add: 'Moderate (not serious) because the change does not remove an existing safety net — it merely fails to add one, so the debt accrues gradually; serious tier is reserved for active regressions (removals, bypasses). Maint lane (not prod) because the harm is primarily a future-maintainability gap rather than a current runtime defect.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit post-rollback state assertion to the plan: describe what a code-v2 reader does with code-v3 sidecars already in storage (e.g., unknown fields are ignored by the Pydantic model and scored as 0) and confirm this is acceptable and lossless.
findings.finding: The plan does not assert the schema/data state after a rollback from code-v3 to code-v2. It states the code-v2 path is 'byte-unchanged for old sidecars' (forward compatibility), but does not address what a code-v2 reader does when it encounters a code-v3 sidecar that carries the new `reachable_path_without_automated_coverage` field — nor does it confirm no data loss or corruption occurs for those already-written records.
findings.location: What section / AC line 3: 'the code-v2 path is byte-unchanged for old sidecars'
findings.suggested_fix: In the AC or 'What' section, add: 'On rollback to code-v2, existing code-v3 sidecars in storage are read with the new field silently dropped (Pydantic extra=ignore / abstain-safe default); impact scores for those sidecars revert to code-v2 logic (no crash, no data loss, but the new signal is lost). This is acceptable because the version-segmented threshold does not consume code-v3 cohort data until the version is stable.' If Pydantic's extra-field behavior is not 'ignore', make it so explicitly.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the concrete dispatch mechanism for version-branched impact_code scoring (e.g., a version kwarg, a `{version: fn}` registry, or explicit statement that old sidecars are never re-scored and the 'byte-unchanged' constraint applies only to the sidecar reader, not the scoring function).
findings.finding: AC line 3 asserts 'the code-v2 path is byte-unchanged for old sidecars', implying version-branched dispatch. However, `impact_code` in `decide.py` is a single unversioned function with no version parameter, and the plan specifies no protocol for how v2 vs v3 routing is to be implemented — a parallel function, a version kwarg, a registry lookup, or something else. Without a specified dispatch mechanism, 'byte-unchanged for old sidecars' is structurally unverifiable as written.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
